perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and the obligations of the Parties hereunder shall terminate two years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and the obligations of the Parties hereunder shall terminate six months from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to six months could violate trade secret protection laws, which often require a longer period to safeguard confidential information. Many states including Texas recognize trade secrets, and reducing protection term is contradiction to trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (TUTSA) - Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.001
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act, reducing the term of confidentiality agreement to only six months from two years may provide insufficient time to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.002, therefore impacting the business's rights regarding trade secrets and intellectual property.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 134A. TRADE SECRETS CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 6. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS CHAPTER 134A. TRADE SECRETS Sec. 134A.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. Sec. 134A.002. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (1) "Claimant" means a party seeking to recover damages under this chapter, including a plaintiff, counterclaimant, cross-claimant, or third-party plaintiff. In an action in which a party seeks recovery of damages under this chapter on behalf of another person, "claimant" includes both that other person and the party seeking recovery of damages. (1-a) "Clear and convincing" means the measure or degree of proof that will produce in the mind of the trier of fact a firm belief or conviction as to the truth of the allegations sought to be established. (2) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, to limit use, or to prohibit discovery of a trade secret, or espionage through electronic or other means. (3) "Misappropriation" means: (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person's knowledge of the trade secret was: (a) derived from or through a person who used improper means to acquire the trade secret; (b) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain the secrecy of or limit the use of the trade secret; or (c) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain the secrecy of or limit the use of the trade secret; or (iii) before a material change of the position of the person, knew or had reason to know that the trade secret was a trade secret and that knowledge of the trade secret
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from two years to six months, which creates a potential contradiction with trade secret protection laws. The Perturbation Explanation states that this reduction could violate trade secret protection laws, particularly concerning the time required to safeguard confidential information, in states like Texas. The Law Explanation supports this, arguing that six months may be insufficient to protect information qualifying as a trade secret under Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.002. Scraped Law Snippet 1, derived from the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act, defines 'Misappropriation' which involves the acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret by improper means, emphasizing the importance of maintaining secrecy. By reducing the confidentiality term, the changed text weakens the ability to prevent misappropriation, potentially contradicting the purpose of the trade secret act.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines that a Party or any of its Representatives have breached Page 4 of 5 this Agreement, then such Party shall be liable and pay to the other Party the reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred by the other Party in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, each Party shall bear its own legal fees and expenses, regardless of the outcome of the litigation.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that allows the prevailing party to recover legal fees can be problematic, especially if a breach involves gross misconduct or willful infringement of confidentiality. This might disincentivize parties from pursuing legitimate claims, potentially undermining contractual obligations. In New York, where this contract is to be performed, denying attorneys' fees in certain circumstances might conflict with the principle of making the injured party whole.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 8101
perturbation.law_explanation: While NY CPLR § 8101 generally discusses costs in an action, altering a contractual provision for attorney fees, especially in cases involving demonstrable harm from breach, runs contrary to the common law principle in New York of compensating the injured party for their losses, which often include legal expenses. This is especially true if the agreement specifies remedies for breach.

Costs in an action. The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that a party found to have breached the agreement in litigation is liable for the other party's legal fees. The changed text states that each party bears its own legal fees, regardless of the litigation's outcome. This directly contradicts the principle outlined in NY CPLR § 8101, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that 'The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.' The modified text removes the potential for the prevailing party to recover costs, thus creating an inconsistency with established legal principles of compensating a prevailing party for the costs incurred in litigation, undermining the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party expressly agrees to waive the defense that a remedy in damages will be adequate, and agrees to use its reasonable best efforts to cause its Representatives to waive, any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with any such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party expressly agrees to maintain the defense that a remedy in damages will be adequate, regardless of the nature of the breach of contract.
perturbation.explanation: Maintaining the defense that a remedy in damages will be adequate regardless of the breach type, contradicts the specific performance and injunctive relief that a plaintiff may be awarded. This is a misrepresentation of injunctive relief and specific performance in the state of New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: Under NY CPLR § 6301, a preliminary injunction may be granted in actions where monetary damages are insufficient, such as cases involving irreparable harm or violation of unique contractual obligations. This modification explicitly maintains that damages are adequate in all cases, contradicting the availability of injunctive relief under New York law and limiting the remedies available for breach of contract.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that a remedy in damages will always be adequate, which contradicts NY CPLR § 6301 as described in the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet from NYSenate.gov states that a preliminary injunction may be granted where monetary damages are insufficient, particularly in cases involving irreparable harm. By asserting that damages are always adequate, the modified contract text eliminates the possibility of injunctive relief, directly contradicting the law's provision for such relief when damages are insufficient.
